summary: When I arrived in America, I first landed in San Francisco. It was on the 4th of July, and I remember seeing fireworks celebrating Independence Day. After disembarking there, everyone went their separate ways to join their husbands or families in different places like Stockton or Fresno. San Francisco was the gateway for many Japanese immigrants like myself, but soon we dispersed to smaller communities.

I did not stop in Hawaii when I came to America; my journey took me directly to Seattle. I remember hearing about Hawaii and how long the boat trip might take, but for me, it was about nine days to Hawaii and sixteen days altogether to Seattle. I was so seasick on the boat that I swore I would never want to ride a boat again. Hawaii was just a place I passed by on my way to the mainland.

Seattle was my first stop in America after the long boat trip. From there, we went on to Portland, Oregon, where my husband worked in a store. Seattle was a key point in our journey, marking the beginning of our new life in America. It was a bustling city compared to the rural places we later lived in.

I wanted to come to America so much, though when I arrived, it was not what I imagined. My husband warned me that America was like a big mountain compared to Mt. Fuji, and indeed, it was desolate and lonely at first. We settled in places like Stockton, Marysville, and East Sacramento, and life was hard, especially with language barriers and homesickness. Despite the struggles, we worked hard for our children’s future and felt grateful for the opportunities here.

I was born in Okayama-ken, Taka-machi, Japan, and left from Yokohama to come to America. Back in Japan, people discouraged me from going, saying I should stay and find work there. I returned to Japan once when my eldest child was one, before the 1924 immigration law was passed, but eventually came back to America for my children’s sake. Japan was my home, and I often thought about it, especially the culture and language I wanted my children to know.

Before coming to America, my husband told me that if I hadn’t climbed Mount Fuji, I hadn’t really seen anything. He meant that America would be a big challenge, like a mountain to climb. When I arrived, I understood what he meant because America felt desolate and difficult compared to Japan. Mount Fuji was a symbol of home and strength that prepared me for the hardships ahead.

Stockton was the first place we settled after arriving in America. We lived there for about a year before moving on. It was one of the early Japanese communities where many immigrants gathered, but life was still lonely and hard as we struggled with language and finding work. Stockton was the starting point of our American journey.

After Stockton, we moved to Marysville, where we stayed for two or three years. Marysville had a Japanese community and was one of the places where Japanese laborers lived and worked. Life there was still challenging, but it was part of our journey to establish ourselves in America. We later moved to East Sacramento from Marysville.

Eventually, we settled in East Sacramento, where we ran a grocery store. This was a more stable time for us, and we stayed there for a while. East Sacramento was a place where we could work hard and provide for our children. It represented a step forward in our life in America.

The Nisei, or second-generation Japanese Americans, grew up with better education and opportunities than the Issei. I noticed that most Nisei marry for love, unlike our generation which had arranged or picture-bride marriages. The Nisei had a strong sense of responsibility and grew up in spacious country environments, which helped them avoid trouble. They are doing well now compared to the hardships the Issei faced.

The Issei were the first-generation Japanese immigrants like my husband and me. Most of us came with little education and worked hard in farm or domestic jobs. Our marriages were mostly arranged or picture-bride marriages, and life was very difficult, especially with language barriers. Despite hardships, Issei mothers prioritized their children’s success and wellbeing above all.

The Sansei, or third-generation Japanese Americans, seem to be getting too much attention and are more spoiled in my opinion. Their parents are well educated and provide them with everything, leading to a more selfish standard of living. This is very different from the Issei and Nisei generations who had to struggle and work hard. I worry that the Sansei may not appreciate the sacrifices made by earlier generations.

We left Japan from Yokohama on the 4th of July, which was America’s Independence Day. I remember seeing fireworks as we departed, and my husband explained the significance of the day to me. Yokohama was the port where many Japanese immigrants began their journey to America, including us. It was a bittersweet farewell to home.

We left Yokohama on the 4th of July, and I saw fireworks celebrating America’s Independence Day. My husband told me about the holiday and how important it was to Americans. It was a memorable start to our journey, symbolizing freedom and new beginnings in a strange land.

Seattle was my first stop in America after the long boat trip. From there, we went on to Portland, Oregon, where my husband worked in a store. Seattle was a key point in our journey, marking the beginning of our new life in America. It was a bustling city compared to the rural places we later lived in.

After arriving in Seattle, we moved on to Portland, Oregon, where my husband worked in a store. Later, we were taken to work on the railroad in the mountains inland, where the foreman was Japanese. Portland was an early stop in our American life before moving to more rural and isolated areas. It was part of the transition from city to country living.

While working on the railroad in the mountains inland, the cook was from Kumamoto Prefecture. Sometimes he would speak in the Kumamoto dialect, which we couldn’t understand. It reminded me of the diversity within Japan itself, even among Japanese immigrants. Kumamoto was a small but memorable part of our experience in America.

When I returned to Japan before the 1924 immigration law, it was during the Taishō period. At that time, there wasn’t much of a depression in Japan, though a tragedy occurred in the Tokyo Kanto area a year later. The economy was not as bad as it is now, and life was relatively stable. The Taishō period was a time of hope before the difficulties that followed.

I came back to America before the 1924 immigration act was passed, which later stopped picture-bride marriages. Before that law, many Japanese couples came together to America, but after it was harder to bring wives over. This law changed the immigration patterns for Japanese families like mine. It was a significant event affecting our community.

There was a tragedy in the Tokyo Kanto area a year after I returned to Japan during the Taishō period. It was not a depression but a significant event that affected the region. Tokyo was a major city that experienced hardships even then, though the economy was still better than it is now. The memory of Tokyo’s challenges stayed with me.

The Kanto region, including Tokyo, suffered a tragedy a year after I returned to Japan during the Taishō period. It was a difficult time but not a full economic depression. The event affected many people and was part of the history I remember from Japan before coming back to America. The Kanto region was central to Japan’s challenges then.

As a Japanese American, I experienced the challenges of being Issei, the first generation, working hard and raising children in a new country. We faced language barriers and cultural differences, but we wanted our children, the Nisei, to succeed and become outstanding citizens. The Japanese American community was close-knit but also spread out in places like Stockton, Marysville, and Sacramento. Our identity was shaped by both Japanese heritage and American life.
